steps.title: Identify the two clauses being joined
steps.content:
The sentence has two independent clauses: (1) "At 1,377 meters, the Tsing Ma Bridge in China is one of the longest suspension bridges in the ______" and (2) "the Golden Gate Bridge in the United States, at 1,280 meters, is even longer." The second clause contrasts with the first, so a coordinating conjunction showing contrast ("but") is needed. When joining two independent clauses with a coordinating conjunction, a comma must come before it.
  • Choice A — 'world but': Missing the comma before "but." Standard English requires a comma before a coordinating conjunction joining two independent clauses. ✗
  • Choice B — 'world, but,': Adds an extra comma after "but," which incorrectly separates the conjunction from the clause it introduces. ✗
  • Choice C — 'world,': Omits the conjunction entirely, creating a comma splice — two independent clauses joined only by a comma. ✗
  • Choice D — 'world, but': Correctly places a comma before "but," joining the two independent clauses with the proper coordinating conjunction showing contrast. ✓

steps.title: Confirm the rule and select
steps.content: Two independent clauses joined by a coordinating conjunction require a comma before the conjunction. "But" signals the needed contrast (the Golden Gate is longer despite the Tsing Ma being one of the longest). Choice D is the only option with the correct comma placement and no extra punctuation. That matches choice D — D is correct.
